column1: Gillian's parents were both musicians, her dad a singer and her mum a violinist. She had realised from a young age that shr hadn't inherited her dad's voice, bit she had quite a talent_______________playing instruments.

So, when Gillian was seven years old, her mum arranged for her to take violin lessons. The teacher was astonished at how skilful Gillian was. "In___________probability, she will be a huge star one day," her teacher remarked.

By age 11, Gillian was performing in her town's orchestra and became a featured soloist by age 14. She______________so much time and effort to her violin studies that her parents felt she wasn't studying enough.
"I hate to___________the bad news to you, but you're going to have a read more and play less," her mum told her.
Gillian, a high-achieving student as school, felt discouraged___________this news. She loved play by violin mote than everything in the world.

Because of this, she found herself in a quite dilemma. Should she abandon her lifetime________________to perform onstage, or should she hold onto it and just let her school marks be above average, rather than excellent?

As much as she felt she had a responsibility to her studies, she chose the latter. She_____________at this decision by telling herself that being on above-average student was something to be very proud of, but being a professional violinist was a dream come true.
